title: Model Size, Speed, and Accuracy Trade-Offs
subtitle: Small language model work is the art of buying enough reasoning quality with far less latency, memory, and infrastructure.
summary: The SLM research project studies small-model optimization, post-training, quantization, pruning, and HPC execution for math reasoning.

artifact.title: Accuracy is not the only axis
artifact.description: Move the model-size slider to see how quality, latency, and memory pull against each other.

contentBlocks.text: A large model may win on peak benchmark accuracy, but a smaller model can win the product if it is fast enough to run interactively, cheap enough to call often, and controllable enough to fine-tune or deploy near the data.

contentBlocks.content:
Post-trainingImprove behavior after pretraining with supervised examples or preference signals.
QuantizationStore weights in fewer bits to reduce memory and improve throughput.
PruningRemove less useful structure to shrink compute.
DistillationTrain a smaller model to imitate a stronger teacher.

sections.heading: The Deployment Equation
body: A model is not just a score. It is a bundle of memory footprint, context length, latency, batch throughput, hardware availability, calibration, and failure modes.
For math reasoning, the key question is not only whether the model can solve hard problems, but whether post-training improves systematic reasoning rather than memorized answer patterns.

sections.heading: Optimization Stack
body: Quantization changes numeric representation. Pruning changes model structure. Fine-tuning changes behavior. Evaluation decides whether any of those changes helped.

The dangerous part is that each optimization can improve one metric while damaging another. A smaller model can become faster but less calibrated; a fine-tuned model can improve one benchmark while narrowing its generality.

rows: Quality loss if precision is too low

pitfalls: Reporting only accuracy without latency or memory.
Evaluating on the same distribution used for fine-tuning.
Optimizing average latency while ignoring tail latency.
Confusing benchmark gains with robust reasoning gains.

quiz.question: Why might a smaller model be preferable despite lower peak accuracy?
options: It may be faster, cheaper, and easier to deploy
quiz.explanation: Real systems balance quality with latency, cost, memory, and deployment constraints.
